1: A shadow: The flying scroll — the curse of the law — is the document of human sin and its consequences. Christ becomes the curse that the scroll pronounces: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The scroll that 'goes out over the face of the whole land' (v3) condemning thieves and false swearers is the comprehensive indictment of human sin; Christ absorbs this condemnation: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us' (2 Corinthians 5:21).
2: A shadow: The enormous flying scroll (20 × 10 cubits — the same dimensions as the temple porch) proclaims the curse from the sanctuary. The law's curse (the dimensions of the sanctuary reversed from blessing to judgment) finds its resolution in Christ, who enters the sanctuary not to pronounce curse but to offer the definitive sacrifice: 'He entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:12).
3: A shadow: The curse against stealing (the eighth commandment) and false swearing (the third commandment) represents the full law's condemnation of human sin. Christ fulfills the law in its fullness and bears its curse in its fullness: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). The two commandments on the scroll represent both tables of the law — toward neighbor and toward God — both of which Christ satisfies.
4: A shadow: The curse that enters the violator's house and consumes its timber and stones — a self-executing divine judgment that destroys the physical structure — is the judgment Christ absorbs at the cross. The structure being destroyed by the curse is the temple itself (the curse entering the house of YHWH, v4b): Jesus drives out the merchants and predicts the temple's destruction (Matthew 21:12-13; Zechariah 24:2). The curse that should destroy the sinner destroys the sanctuary — fulfilled when the Temple veil is torn at Christ's death (Matthew 27:51) and the Temple is destroyed in 70 CE.
5: A shadow: The seventh vision (the woman in the ephah) begins with a summons to see 'what is going out.' The removal of wickedness from the land is the work Christ accomplishes by removing the power of sin: 'He appeared in order to take away sins, and in him there is no sin' (1 John 3:5). The sin that is removed from the land and sent to Babylon (its proper home) is the sin that Christ, the Lamb of God, takes away: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (John 1:29).
6: A shadow: The wickedness contained in the measuring-basket (eifah) is the sin that Christ contains and removes. The gospel's transfer of sin: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). The ephah-as-sin-container is a type of the cross as the site where sin is contained and dealt with — Christ bearing the weight of human wickedness, sealed in the container of his atoning death.
7: A shadow: The woman (wickedness personified) sealed under the lead cover in the ephah is the binding of the power of sin. Christ at the resurrection seals the power of sin and death: 'Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Corinthians 15:54-57). The sealed wickedness will be permanently confined; the resurrection seals death's defeat.
8: A shadow: The angel forcing wickedness back into the container and sealing it is a type of Christ's binding of the strong man: 'How can someone enter a strong man's house and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong man?' (Matthew 12:29). Christ's exorcisms in the Gospel are the binding of wickedness; his death and resurrection are the definitive sealing of the power of evil. The angel's forceful action in Zechariah's vision foreshadows the eschatological binding of Satan in Revelation 20:2-3.
9: A shadow: The two women with stork wings transporting wickedness away from the land are a type of the angelic agents of divine judgment who execute Christ's purification of the earth. The harvest parable: 'The Son of Man will send his angels, and they will gather out of his kingdom all causes of sin and all law-breakers' (Matthew 13:41). The removal of the ephah to Shinar is the harvest judgment that Christ's coming accomplishes.
10: A shadow: The destination of wickedness is Shinar/Babylon — where sin returns to its proper home, away from the covenant land. The eschatological purification of the new earth: 'Nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false, but only those who are written in the Lamb's book of life' (Revelation 21:27). The removal of the ephah to Babylon anticipates the separation of the holy city from Babylon in Revelation: the new Jerusalem descends from heaven while Babylon falls (Rev 17-21).
11: A shadow: The house built for wickedness in Shinar — its permanent residence — is the 'outer darkness' where all that opposes God is finally contained. Christ's parable of the weeds: 'The weeds are gathered and burned with fire. So will it be at the end of the age' (Matthew 13:40). The wickedness that was removed from the covenant land is established in its permanent domain; the purified earth is the inheritance of the righteous. The ephah removed to Shinar is the NT's wicked cast 'into the lake of fire' (Revelation 20:14-15).
